intro: Togglz is free and open source while Bucket runs on paid subscription pricing. Bucket: Feature Flags / Remote Config tool for developers. Specializes in Feature Management. Togglz: Open-source Java feature toggle library implementing the Feature Toggles pattern, using Java enums for type-safe flag definitions. Apache 2.0 licensed. Bucket fits gradual rollouts to reduce risk. Togglz fits JVM monoliths wanting simple, type-safe in-process flags. On our rubric Bucket scores 4/5 for developer experience and 4/5 for transparency, while Togglz scores 4/5 and 5/5. The honest trade-off: Bucket's main drawback — Solo developer shipping to production; Togglz's — Java/JVM-only ecosystem. For Togglz: Type-safe Java enums for flag definitions. Bucket is explicitly not the right pick for solo projects. Togglz is not aimed at polyglot systems or experimentation-heavy product teams. Another Bucket advantage: Kill switches for features. Another Togglz advantage: Long-standing Apache 2.0 Java toggle library. Another Bucket friction point: Products with no users yet. Another Togglz friction point: Less innovation recently vs new entrants.
quick_take: Bucket is for gradual rollouts to reduce risk; Togglz is for JVM monoliths wanting simple, type-safe in-process flags; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Bucket if gradual rollouts to reduce risk, and if you'd rather pay for a managed service than run infrastructure yourself.
verdict_b: Choose Togglz if JVM monoliths wanting simple, type-safe in-process flags, and if you want to avoid recurring license costs and are willing to self-host.

faq.a: No: Togglz is free and open source, while Bucket runs on paid subscription pricing. Bucket may still be cheaper in total cost once you factor in hosting, maintenance, and engineering time to run Togglz yourself.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Bucket to Togglz?
faq.a: Moving to Togglz is reasonable on its side — it's low lock-in (Open source Java library). The friction is on the way out of Bucket, which is medium lock-in (Feature flag platform). Budget time for data export and integration rework.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, Bucket or Togglz?
faq.a: Both score 4/5 for developer experience in our rubric, so neither has a structural edge. The practical answer depends on stack fit: Bucket's ergonomics suit some workflows, Togglz's suit others. Try both on a throwaway project before committing.

faq.q: Is Togglz a good alternative to Bucket?
faq.a: They sit in the same category, so yes — Togglz is a plausible alternative for many Bucket use cases. It fits best when JVM monoliths wanting simple, type-safe in-process flags. Skip it if polyglot systems or experimentation-heavy product teams.
